But as a collaborative approach, we all tried to do our best in the remaining time.

column17:
Yes this workshop has helped a lot and the experience and practical exposure which I got in these 3 days is something which I didn't experience even in the 3 years of college. 
There are certain issue around ourselves, in our society and we all had knowledge about that. But, we think little about to my come from our comfort zone and do something at the grass root level for our society and people.
But this workshop helped me to look back at all those issues which we see at daily basis but we didn't take any action, and even we don't much on that, hum sochte hain ki hmesha se chal rha aur aage bhi chalta rahega hum kya kr skte h isme.

When rohit ask a problem "Suppose that in a world full of resources, what one problem you will solve "
And jab apne kaha ki kuch bhi ho skti h problem jo ap apne aas pas dekhte ho ya jaha se ap belong krte ho,
His words suddenly strikes my mind, about  Domestic Violence.
Me bachpan se dekh rhi hu Domestic violence mere ghr me aas pas, and jab kbhi aankhein band krte h wo sab soch ke, me andar se bilkul hil jati hu, wo kya huya tha kyu huya tha, aur bura lgta tha ki me kuch nhi kr payi kyunki me choti thi bhahut.

column17:
Yes, this workshop has definitely helped to deepen my understanding of justice challenges. Earlier, I mainly viewed justice from a theoretical and legal perspective, but through this workshop I understood the practical barriers people face, such as lack of legal awareness, delays in dispute resolution, financial constraints, and limited access to mechanisms like ADR and mediation.

It also helped me realise the importance of innovation, community engagement, and collaborative problem-solving in making the justice system more accessible and effective. Overall, the workshop enhanced my ability to analyse real-life justice issues with a more practical, empathetic, and solution-oriented approach.

column43:
The most valuable “aha moment” I am taking away from Justice Innovation 101 is the realization that justice is not only about laws and courts, but also about accessibility, awareness, and practical solutions for people. I understood that many justice challenges exist not because laws are absent, but because people lack knowledge, resources, or trust in the system.

This workshop helped me see the importance of innovation, ADR mechanisms, and community-based approaches in bridging this gap. It changed my perspective from viewing justice as a purely legal process to understanding it as a people-centric system that requires empathy, collaboration, and creative problem-solving.
column44:
The most helpful tool I learnt during the workshop to define the problem was problem mapping (or problem tree analysis). This method helped me clearly identify the root causes, key stakeholders, and real impact of the justice issue instead of just focusing on surface-level symptoms.

By breaking the problem into causes and effects, I was able to understand the issue more systematically, prioritise the main concerns, and think of practical and targeted solutions. It also improved my analytical thinking and helped our team stay focused on the actual problem we wanted to address.
